tldr: Headlines worldwide lead with two interlocking signals: Ukraine's appointment of new commander-in-chief Mykhailo Drapatyi replacing General Syrskyi (military succession angle), and the political standoff between Zelensky and ousted defence minister Fedorov who refuses any role other than his old post. A third layer — street protests as the force that triggered the shake-up — appears in several outlets; Russian-exile media alone frames the appointment through Moscow's punitive geopolitical response.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline to describe the military reshuffle as a 'purge' — the sole title-level use of a term carrying authoritarian connotations that no other outlet applied to the personnel changes.

Only headline to use the nickname 'The Butcher' for the departing general Syrskyi, surfacing a moral-accountability signal about his casualty record at the title level that other outlets reserved for body text.

Only headline to foreground Russia's arrest warrant response within hours of Drapatyi's appointment, making Moscow's punitive geopolitical reaction the headline angle rather than anything about the new commander's qualifications.

Only headline to quote Drapatyi's maximal statement that Russia 'has no right to exist,' framing the new commander through an explicit ideological-existential lens absent from every other outlet's title.

bottom_line: At headline level, coverage is dominated by military succession imagery (who replaces Syrsky) and political standoff framing (Fedorov refuses to back down), while the deeper institutional civilian-military tensions visible in body-text analysis remain largely absent from titles.
